text: Huntington's disease (HD) is a neurodegenerative disorder caused by the aggregation of the mutant huntingtin (mHTT) protein in nerve cells.

meta.source_title: Molecular Strategies to Target Protein Aggregation in Huntington's Disease.

text: mHTT self-aggregates to form soluble oligomers and insoluble fibrils, which interfere in a number of key cellular functions.

meta.source_title: Molecular Strategies to Target Protein Aggregation in Huntington's Disease.

text: This leads to cell quiescence and ultimately cell death.

meta.source_title: Molecular Strategies to Target Protein Aggregation in Huntington's Disease.

text: There are currently still no treatments available for HD, but approaches targeting the HTT levels offer systematic, mechanism-driven routes towards curing HD and other neurodegenerative diseases.

meta.source_title: Molecular Strategies to Target Protein Aggregation in Huntington's Disease.

text: These methods include the proteolysis-targeting chimera, Trim-Away, autophagosome-tethering compound, autophagy-targeting chimera, lysosome-targeting chimera and approach targeting mHTT for chaperone-mediated autophagy.

meta.source_title: Molecular Strategies to Target Protein Aggregation in Huntington's Disease.

text: These molecular strategies provide a knowledge-based approach to target HD and other neurodegenerative diseases at the origin.

meta.source_title: Molecular Strategies to Target Protein Aggregation in Huntington's Disease.

text: This review summarizes the current state of knowledge of the mRNA targeting approaches such as antisense oligonucleotides and RNAi system; and the novel methods targeting mHTT and aggregates for degradation via the ubiquitin proteasome or the autophagy-lysosomal systems.

text: Background: Neuroimaging studies of autosomal dominant Alzheimer's disease (ADAD) enable characterization of the trajectories of cerebral amyloid-beta (A beta) and tau accumulation in the decades prior to clinical symptom onset.

meta.source_title: Longitudinal amyloid and tau accumulation in autosomal dominant Alzheimer’s disease: findings from the Colombia-Boston (COLBOS) biomarker study

text: Longitudinal rates of regional tau accumulation measured with positron emission tomography (PET) and their relationship with other biomarker and cognitive changes remain to be fully characterized in ADAD.

meta.source_title: Longitudinal amyloid and tau accumulation in autosomal dominant Alzheimer’s disease: findings from the Colombia-Boston (COLBOS) biomarker study

text: Methods: Fourteen ADAD mutation carriers (Presenilin-1 E280A) and 15 age-matched non-carriers from the Colombian kindred underwent 2-3 sessions of A beta (11C-Pittsburgh compound B) and tau (18F-flortaucipir) PET, structural magnetic resonance imaging, and neuropsychological evaluation over a 2-4-year follow-up period.

meta.source_title: Longitudinal amyloid and tau accumulation in autosomal dominant Alzheimer’s disease: findings from the Colombia-Boston (COLBOS) biomarker study

text: Annualized rates of change for imaging and cognitive variables were compared between carriers and non-carriers, and relationships among baseline measurements and rates of change were assessed within carriers.

meta.source_title: Longitudinal amyloid and tau accumulation in autosomal dominant Alzheimer’s disease: findings from the Colombia-Boston (COLBOS) biomarker study

text: Results: Longitudinal measurements were consistent with a sequence of ADAD-related changes beginning with A beta accumulation (16 years prior to expected symptom onset, EYO), followed by entorhinal cortex (EC) tau (9 EYO), neocortical tau (6 EYO), hippocampal atrophy (6 EYO), and cognitive decline (4 EYO).

meta.source_title: Longitudinal amyloid and tau accumulation in autosomal dominant Alzheimer’s disease: findings from the Colombia-Boston (COLBOS) biomarker study

text: Rates of tau accumulation among carriers were most rapid in parietal neocortex (similar to 9%/year).

meta.source_title: Longitudinal amyloid and tau accumulation in autosomal dominant Alzheimer’s disease: findings from the Colombia-Boston (COLBOS) biomarker study

text: EC tau PET signal at baseline was a significant predictor of subsequent neocortical tau accumulation and cognitive decline within carriers.

meta.source_title: Longitudinal amyloid and tau accumulation in autosomal dominant Alzheimer’s disease: findings from the Colombia-Boston (COLBOS) biomarker study

text: Conclusions: Our results are consistent with the sequence of biological changes in ADAD implied by cross-sectional studies and highlight the importance of EC tau as an early biomarker and a potential link between A beta burden and neocortical tau accumulation in ADAD.

text: This cross-sectional European study evaluates the application of optimized magnetic resonance imaging protocols at 3 Tesla (T) for detecting and distinguishing MS from other conditions that mimic MS among adults.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Importance The central vein sign has been proposed as a specific imaging biomarker for distinguishing between multiple sclerosis (MS) and not MS, mainly based on findings from ultrahigh-field magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) studies.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: The diagnostic value of the central vein sign in a multicenter setting with a variety of clinical 3 tesla (T) MRI protocols, however, remains unknown.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Objective To evaluate the sensitivity and specificity of various central vein sign lesion criteria for differentiating MS from non-MS conditions using 3T brain MRI with various commonly used pulse sequences.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Design, Setting, and Participants This large multicenter, cross-sectional study enrolled participants (n = 648) of ongoing observational studies and patients included in neuroimaging research databases of 8 neuroimaging centers in Europe.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Patient enrollment and MRI data collection were performed between January 1, 2010, and November 30, 2016.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Data analysis was conducted between January 1, 2016, and April 30, 2018.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Investigators were blinded to participant diagnosis by a novel blinding procedure.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Main Outcomes and Measures Occurrence of central vein sign was detected on 3T T2*-weighted or susceptibility-weighted imaging.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Sensitivity and specificity were assessed for these MRI sequences and for different central vein sign lesion criteria, which were defined by the proportion of lesions with central vein sign or by absolute numbers of lesions with central vein sign.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Results A total of 606 participants were included in the study after exclusion of 42 participants.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Among the 606 participants, 413 (68.2%) were women.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Patients with clinically isolated syndrome and relapsing-remitting MS (RRMS) included 235 women (66.6%) and had a median (range) age of 37 (14.7-61.4) years, a median (range) disease duration of 2 (0-33) years, and a median (range) Expanded Disability Status Scale score of 1.5 (0-6.5).

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Patients without MS included 178 women (70.4%) and had a median (range) age of 54 (18-83) years.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: A total of 4447 lesions were analyzed in a total of 487 patients: 690 lesions in 98 participants with clinically isolated syndrome, 2815 lesions in 225 participants with RRMS, 54 lesions in 13 participants with neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder, 54 lesions in 14 participants with systemic lupus erythematosus, 121 lesions in 29 participants with migraine or cluster headache, 240 lesions in 20 participants with diabetes, and 473 lesions in 88 participants with other types of small-vessel disease.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: The sensitivity was 68.1% and specificity was 82.9% for distinguishing MS from not MS using a 35% central vein sign proportion threshold.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: The 3 central vein sign lesion criteria had a sensitivity of 61.9% and specificity of 89.0%.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Sensitivity was higher when an optimized T2*-weighted sequence was used.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Conclusions and Relevance In this study, use of the central vein sign at 3T MRI yielded a high specificity and a moderate sensitivity in differentiating MS from not MS; international, multicenter studies may be needed to ascertain whether the central vein sign-based criteria can accurately detect MS.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Question Is the central vein sign on clinical 3T magnetic resonance imaging a useful biomarker for the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis? Findings In this multicenter cross-sectional study of 4447 lesions in 606 participants, use of a 35% central vein sign proportion threshold yielded a sensitivity of 68.1% and a specificity of 82.9% for distinguishing multiple sclerosis from non-multiple sclerosis.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: The criteria of 3 or more central vein sign lesions had a sensitivity of 61.9% and a specificity of 89.0%.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Meaning The 3T central vein sign-based criteria showed a high specificity in the differentiation between multiple sclerosis and non-multiple sclerosis; future studies may be needed to confirm the applicability of this finding to support the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis in clinical practice.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Background: Fatigue is the major cause of disability in MS.

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: Fatigue has been suggested to be primary, part of the neurological disease; it can also be secondary to other diseases outside the CNS or exist as a separate comorbidity.

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: The only forms of measurement currently available are through subjective standardized questionnaires, which are not able to identify primary MS-related fatigue.

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: Therefore, there is a need for objective biomarkers of fatigue in MS.

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: This study explored the viability of 17 possible biomarkers of primary fatigue in MS.

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: Our chosen biomarker panel represents the function and health of different parts of the CNS.

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: Methods: We evaluated 31 MS patients and 17 healthy controls using the Fatigue Severity Scale (FSS) and Insomnia Severity Index (ISI).

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: We assessed clinical parameters and collected CSF from all participants to analyze 17 biomarkers, some of which in multiple targeted sequences, reflecting structural and functional changes in the brain.

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: Based on FSS scores, MS was divided into MS-Fatigue (MS-F, FSS >= 4) and MS-NoFatigue (MS-NoF, FSS < 4).

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: Results: MS-F had significantly lower levels of amyloid precursor protein (APP) peptides than MS-NoF (p = 0.005, p = 0.011).

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: The only biomarker correlating with FSS in any group was APP in MS (r = -0.47, -0.52; p = 0.007, 0.002).

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: APP did not correlate with any clinical parameter in MS but correlated with multiple markers.

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: In MS, FSS correlated with the ISI and months since diagnosis.

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: Conclusion: Although the mechanisms remain unknown, altered APP metabolism in MS seems to be associated with fatigue.

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: APP should be evaluated as a biomarker of the role of structural MS pathology in the development of fatigue in individual MS patients.

meta.source_title: Cerebrospinal fluid amyloid precursor protein as a potential biomarker of fatigue in multiple sclerosis: A pilot study.

text: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common fatal neurodegenerative disease of the elderly worldwide.

meta.source_title: Alix: A Candidate Serum Biomarker of Alzheimer's Disease.

text: The identification of AD biomarkers will allow for earlier diagnosis and thus earlier intervention.

meta.source_title: Alix: A Candidate Serum Biomarker of Alzheimer's Disease.

text: The aim of this study was to find such biomarkers.

meta.source_title: Alix: A Candidate Serum Biomarker of Alzheimer's Disease.

text: It was observed that the expression of Alix was significantly decreased in brain tissues and serum samples from AD patients compared to the controls.

meta.source_title: Alix: A Candidate Serum Biomarker of Alzheimer's Disease.

text: A significant correlation between Alix levels and cognitive decline was observed (r = 0.80; p < 0.001) as well as a significant negative correlation between Alix and A beta(40) in serum levels (r =-0.60, p < 0.001).

meta.source_title: Alix: A Candidate Serum Biomarker of Alzheimer's Disease.

text: The receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC) analysis showed the area under the curve (AUC) of Alix was 0.80, and the optimal cut-off point of 199.5 pg/ml was selected with the highest sum of sensitivity and specificity.

meta.source_title: Alix: A Candidate Serum Biomarker of Alzheimer's Disease.

text: The diagnostic accuracy for serum Alix was 74%, with 76% sensitivity and 71% specificity respectively, which could differentiate AD from controls.

meta.source_title: Alix: A Candidate Serum Biomarker of Alzheimer's Disease.

text: In addition, the expression of Alix was found to be significantly decreased in AD compared to vascular dementia (VaD).

meta.source_title: Alix: A Candidate Serum Biomarker of Alzheimer's Disease.

text: ROC analysis between AD and VaD showed that the AUC was 0.777, which could be indicative of the role of serum Alix as a biomarker in the differential diagnosis between AD and VaD.

meta.source_title: Alix: A Candidate Serum Biomarker of Alzheimer's Disease.

text: Most surprisingly, the decreased expression of Alix was attenuated after the treatment of Memantine in different AD animal models.

meta.source_title: Alix: A Candidate Serum Biomarker of Alzheimer's Disease.

text: In conclusion, our results indicate the possibility of serum Alix as a novel and non-invasive biomarker for AD for the first time.

meta.source_title: Alix: A Candidate Serum Biomarker of Alzheimer's Disease.

text: BackgroundCognitive decline has been reported in premanifest and manifest Huntington's disease but reliable biomarkers are lacking.

meta.source_title: Retinal thickness as a biomarker of cognitive impairment in manifest Huntington's disease.

text: Inner retinal layer thickness seems to be a good biomarker of cognition in other neurodegenerative diseases.ObjectiveTo explore the relationship between optical coherence tomography-derived metrics and global cognition in Huntington's Disease.MethodsThirty-six patients with Huntington's disease (16 premanifest and 20 manifest) and 36 controls matched by age, sex, smoking status, and hypertension status underwent macular volumetric and peripapillary optical coherence tomography scans.

meta.source_title: Retinal thickness as a biomarker of cognitive impairment in manifest Huntington's disease.

text: Disease duration, motor status, global cognition and CAG repeats were recorded in patients.

meta.source_title: Retinal thickness as a biomarker of cognitive impairment in manifest Huntington's disease.

text: Group differences in imaging parameters and their association with clinical outcomes were analyzed using linear mixed-effect models.ResultsPremanifest and manifest Huntington's disease patients presented thinner retinal external limiting membrane-Bruch's membrane complex, and manifest patients had thinner temporal peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer compared to controls.

meta.source_title: Retinal thickness as a biomarker of cognitive impairment in manifest Huntington's disease.

text: In manifest Huntington's disease, macular thickness was significantly associated with MoCA scores, inner nuclear layer showing the largest regression coefficients.

meta.source_title: Retinal thickness as a biomarker of cognitive impairment in manifest Huntington's disease.

text: This relationship was consistent after adjusting for age, sex, and education and p-value correction with False Discovery Rate.

meta.source_title: Retinal thickness as a biomarker of cognitive impairment in manifest Huntington's disease.

text: None of the retinal variables were related to Unified Huntington's Disease Rating Scale score, disease duration, or disease burden.

meta.source_title: Retinal thickness as a biomarker of cognitive impairment in manifest Huntington's disease.

text: Premanifest patients did not show a significant association between OCT-derived parameters and clinical outcomes in corrected models.ConclusionsIn line with other neurodegenerative diseases, OCT is a potential biomarker of cognitive status in manifest HD.

meta.source_title: Retinal thickness as a biomarker of cognitive impairment in manifest Huntington's disease.

text: Future prospective studies are needed to evaluate OCT as a potential surrogate marker of cognitive decline in HD.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Patients with clinically isolated syndrome and relapsing-remitting MS (RRMS) included 235 women (66.6%) and had a median (range) age of 37 (14.7-61.4) years, a median (range) disease duration of 2 (0-33) years, and a median (range) Expanded Disability Status Scale score of 1.5 (0-6.5).

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Investigators were blinded to participant diagnosis by a novel blinding procedure.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Main Outcomes and Measures Occurrence of central vein sign was detected on 3T T2*-weighted or susceptibility-weighted imaging.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Sensitivity and specificity were assessed for these MRI sequences and for different central vein sign lesion criteria, which were defined by the proportion of lesions with central vein sign or by absolute numbers of lesions with central vein sign.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Results A total of 606 participants were included in the study after exclusion of 42 participants.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Among the 606 participants, 413 (68.2%) were women.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Patients with clinically isolated syndrome and relapsing-remitting MS (RRMS) included 235 women (66.6%) and had a median (range) age of 37 (14.7-61.4) years, a median (range) disease duration of 2 (0-33) years, and a median (range) Expanded Disability Status Scale score of 1.5 (0-6.5).

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Patients without MS included 178 women (70.4%) and had a median (range) age of 54 (18-83) years.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: A total of 4447 lesions were analyzed in a total of 487 patients: 690 lesions in 98 participants with clinically isolated syndrome, 2815 lesions in 225 participants with RRMS, 54 lesions in 13 participants with neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder, 54 lesions in 14 participants with systemic lupus erythematosus, 121 lesions in 29 participants with migraine or cluster headache, 240 lesions in 20 participants with diabetes, and 473 lesions in 88 participants with other types of small-vessel disease.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: The sensitivity was 68.1% and specificity was 82.9% for distinguishing MS from not MS using a 35% central vein sign proportion threshold.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: The 3 central vein sign lesion criteria had a sensitivity of 61.9% and specificity of 89.0%.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Sensitivity was higher when an optimized T2*-weighted sequence was used.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Conclusions and Relevance In this study, use of the central vein sign at 3T MRI yielded a high specificity and a moderate sensitivity in differentiating MS from not MS; international, multicenter studies may be needed to ascertain whether the central vein sign-based criteria can accurately detect MS.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Question Is the central vein sign on clinical 3T magnetic resonance imaging a useful biomarker for the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis? Findings In this multicenter cross-sectional study of 4447 lesions in 606 participants, use of a 35% central vein sign proportion threshold yielded a sensitivity of 68.1% and a specificity of 82.9% for distinguishing multiple sclerosis from non-multiple sclerosis.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: The criteria of 3 or more central vein sign lesions had a sensitivity of 61.9% and a specificity of 89.0%.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of the Central Vein Sign as a Diagnostic Imaging Biomarker in Multiple Sclerosis.

text: Meaning The 3T central vein sign-based criteria showed a high specificity in the differentiation between multiple sclerosis and non-multiple sclerosis; future studies may be needed to confirm the applicability of this finding to support the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis in clinical practice.

text: Huntington's disease is an autosomal dominant hereditary neurodegenerative disease characterized by progressive dystonia, chorea and cognitive or psychiatric disturbances.

meta.source_title: Bioinformatic analysis of a microRNA regulatory network in Huntington's disease.

text: The leading cause is the Huntington gene mutation on the patient's chromosome 4 that produces a mutated protein.

meta.source_title: Bioinformatic analysis of a microRNA regulatory network in Huntington's disease.

text: Recently, attention has focused on the relationship between microRNAs and Huntington's disease's pathogenesis.

meta.source_title: Bioinformatic analysis of a microRNA regulatory network in Huntington's disease.

text: In Huntington's disease, microRNAs can interact with various transcription factors; dysregulated microRNAs may be associated with the Cytosine deoxynucleotide-Adenine ribonucleotides-Guanine ribonucleotide length and Huntington's disease's progression and severity.

meta.source_title: Bioinformatic analysis of a microRNA regulatory network in Huntington's disease.

text: This study explores the role of microRNAs in the pathogenesis of Huntington's disease through bioinformatics analysis.

meta.source_title: Bioinformatic analysis of a microRNA regulatory network in Huntington's disease.

text: By analyzing data from the Gene Expression Omnibus database, we identified a total of 9 differentially expressed microRNA.

meta.source_title: Bioinformatic analysis of a microRNA regulatory network in Huntington's disease.

text: Subsequently, target genes and long non-coding RNAs were predicted, and a comprehensive regulatory network centered on microRNA was constructed.

meta.source_title: Bioinformatic analysis of a microRNA regulatory network in Huntington's disease.

text: The microRNA integrated regulatory network, Homo sapiens (hsa)-miR-144-3p, interacted with the largest number of long non-coding RNAs, including X-inactive specific transcript and taurine upregulated gene 1.

meta.source_title: Bioinformatic analysis of a microRNA regulatory network in Huntington's disease.

text: The miRNAs, hsa-miR-10b-5p and hsa-miR-196a-5p, regulated most of the target genes, including class I homeobox and brain-derived neurotrophic factor genes.

meta.source_title: Bioinformatic analysis of a microRNA regulatory network in Huntington's disease.

text: Additionally, 59 Gene Ontology terms and eight enrichment pathways were identified by analyzing the target genes of hsa-miR-196a-5p and hsa-microRNA-10b-5p.

meta.source_title: Bioinformatic analysis of a microRNA regulatory network in Huntington's disease.

text: In conclusion, hsa-miR-10b-5p and hsa-miR-196a-5p were significantly and differentially expressed in Huntington's disease, the long non-coding RNAs X-inactive specific transcript, taurine upregulated gene 1, and target genes such as homeobox or brain-derived neurotrophic factor may play critical roles in the pathogenesis of Huntington's disease.

text: Huntingtin-associated protein 1 (HAP1), the first identified HTT-binding partner, is highly expressed in the central nervous system, and has been found to associated with neurological diseases.

meta.source_title: Huntingtin-associated protein 1-associated intracellular trafficking in neurodegenerative diseases.

text: Mounting evidence suggests that HAP1 functions as a component of cargo-motor molecules to bind various proteins and participates in intracellular trafficking.

meta.source_title: Huntingtin-associated protein 1-associated intracellular trafficking in neurodegenerative diseases.

text: It is known that the failure of intracellular transport is a key contributor to the progression of neurodegenerative disorders (NDs) including Alzheimer's disease (AD), Huntington's disease (HD), Parkinson's disease (PD), amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), spinal and bulbar muscular atrophy (SBMA) and spinocerebellar ataxia (SCA).

text: The link between HAP1 and various NDs is supported by growing evidence.

text: Our aim was to discuss the role of blood glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) to evaluate clinical severity in patients with HD.Methods: Fifty-seven HD mutation carriers (15 premanifest HD, preHD, and 42 manifest HD) and 26 healthy controls were recruited.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of Blood Glial Fibrillary Acidic Protein as a Potential Marker in Huntington's Disease.

text: Demographic data and clinical severity assessed with the internationally Unified Huntington's Disease Rating Scale (UHDRS) were retrospectively analyzed.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of Blood Glial Fibrillary Acidic Protein as a Potential Marker in Huntington's Disease.

text: Plasma NfL and GFAP were quantified with an ultra-sensitive single-molecule (Simoa, Norcross, GA, USA) technology.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of Blood Glial Fibrillary Acidic Protein as a Potential Marker in Huntington's Disease.

text: We explored their consistency and their correlation with clinical severity.Results: Compared with healthy controls, plasma NfL (p < 0.0001) and GFAP (p < 0.001) were increased in Chinese HD mutation carriers, and they were linearly correlated with each other (r = 0.612, p < 0.001).

meta.source_title: Evaluation of Blood Glial Fibrillary Acidic Protein as a Potential Marker in Huntington's Disease.

text: They were also significantly correlated with disease burden, Total Motor Score (TMS) and Total Functional Capacity (TFC).

meta.source_title: Evaluation of Blood Glial Fibrillary Acidic Protein as a Potential Marker in Huntington's Disease.

text: The scores of Stroop word reading, symbol digit modalities tests, and short version of the Problem Behaviors Assessments (PBAs) for HD were correlated with plasma NfL but not GFAP.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of Blood Glial Fibrillary Acidic Protein as a Potential Marker in Huntington's Disease.

text: Compared with healthy controls, plasma NfL has been increased since stage 1 but plasma GFAP began to increase statistically in stage 2.Conclusions: Plasma GFAP was correlated with plasma NfL, disease burden, TMS, and TFC in HD mutation carriers.

meta.source_title: Evaluation of Blood Glial Fibrillary Acidic Protein as a Potential Marker in Huntington's Disease.

text: Plasma GFAP may have potential to be a sensitive biomarker for evaluating HD progression.
